sn47.37:1.1: Near Sāvatthī. “Monks, there are these four establishings of mindfulness.
sn47.37:1.3: “There is the case where a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.37:1.4: For him, remaining focused on the body in & of itself, any desire for the body is abandoned.
sn47.37:1.5: From the abandoning of desire, the deathless is realized.
sn47.37:2.1: “He remains focused on feelings in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.37:2.2: For him, remaining focused on feelings in & of themselves, any desire for feelings is abandoned.
sn47.37:2.3: From the abandoning of desire, the deathless is realized.
sn47.37:3.1: “He remains focused on the mind in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.37:3.2: For him, remaining focused on the mind in & of itself, any desire for the mind is abandoned.
sn47.37:3.3: From the abandoning of desire, the deathless is realized.
sn47.37:4.1: “He remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.37:4.2: For him, remaining focused on mental qualities in & of themselves, any desire for mental qualities is abandoned.
sn47.37:4.3: From the abandoning of desire, the deathless is realized.”
